text: Hello friends, this patient has large intramural myoma that is approximately eighteen centimeter intramural myoma and we will perform the laparoscopic myomectomy.
So let us see how to perform and this is the port position will be according to baseball diamond concept.

The surgeon introduces a varus needle and trocar through Palmer's point due to the patient's previous caesarean section. After entry, an epigastric port is placed, and adhesiolysis is performed to separate adhesions resulting from the previous surgery.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, carefully separating adhesions formed due to a previous caesarean section. The adhesions are gradually being separated using laparoscopic instruments.

The surgeon has completed the removal of omental adhesions, revealing the left adnexal structures including the round ligament, fallopian tube, and ovary, which appear normal. The inspection proceeds to the right side.
The surgeon prepares to inject vasopressin into the large intramural myoma, which is visible in the surgical field.

Hydro dissection is initiated, visibly causing blabbing and swelling around the myoma. The procedure involves using five international units of vasopressin diluted in twenty milliliters of saline to create a clear dissection plane and induce ischemia, minimizing bleeding.
After injecting vasopressin, the surgeon removes the aspiration needle and proceeds to make an incision on the muscle and serous layer using a harmonic scalpel.

Using an ipsilateral port, the surgeon makes an oblique incision through the seromuscular layer to access the myoma, ensuring the fallopian tube remains undisturbed. The incision technique facilitates easier suturing and avoids damage to the intramural part of the tube.

Achieving a good plane is crucial for the myomectomy surgery because if you get a wrong plane your bleeding also increases and you will not enucleate the entire myoma. So it is better to do the good dissection to achieve the plane.
The surgeon enlarges the incision to accommodate the approximately eighteen-centimeter myoma, revealing a well-defined plane with the muscle already cut and the capsule clearly visible.

The surgeon uses a pushing and pulling technique to manipulate the myoma, periodically repositioning the myoma screw to the base for effective traction. This allows the myoma to be rolled and enucleated with proper traction, ensuring it is not positioned at the fundus where traction would be less effective.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to enucleate the myoma, achieving a good plane for dissection. A self-manufactured stainless steel myoma rod, which is blunt and atraumatic, is then employed to provide strong force for enucleation without the risk of bending.
The surgeon relocates the myoma screw to the base of the myoma, removing it from the apex to ensure effective traction during enucleation.
The surgeon begins dissection at the established plane, smoothly stripping the myoma from surrounding tissue, indicating a successful enucleation process.
The surgeon relocates the myoma screw to the base of the myoma. A superficial muscle layer over the myoma is cut to facilitate its removal, revealing a clearer plane for enucleation.

The surgeon reaches the base of the myoma, where the diameter of the vessels is larger, potentially causing bleeding. Energy devices such as bipolar or ligature are used judiciously to manage the vessels and minimize bleeding.
The surgeon has nearly reached the base of the myoma, with 95% of the tissue already separated. Only a small portion, approximately 5%, remains attached and needs to be cleared.

The surgeon carefully separates the remaining attachment of the myoma using a harmonic scalpel, ensuring the tip does not contact the underlying small bowel to avoid thermal injury. The final detachment is completed, and the myoma is fully separated.
The surgeon begins suturing by introducing the needle through the lower see dust layer into the muscle layer, initiating muscle-to-muscle suturing.
The surgeon begins suturing the intramural muscle layers to obliterate the dead space and control minor bleeding, continuing the muscle-to-muscle suturing technique.
The surgeon removes the needle from the upper end of the serosal layer and prepares to resume suturing.
The surgeon identifies the serous layer and performs shear-to-shear suturing to secure the tissue layers.
The surgeon uses the left hand to apply traction and hold the suture, ensuring it remains tightened while taking a bite into the tissue.

here we have taken all the continuous suturing but there are many options you can use barb suture you can use baseball suturing if you will search then you will find out our many videos with the baseball suture of myomectomy and then in some of the cases we have used the barb suture some cases we have used the extracorporeal square knot and extracorporeal Western knot so depending upon the situation and how much bleeding is there and how much you have the traction in between the tissue you can use a various type of the knots but here there was no bleeding and a good plane myoma is removed so we have done all continuous suturing that is a fast method of closure and it is effective also
The surgeon performs suction irrigation to remove blood clots from the muscle tissue, ensuring the surgical field is clear of any clots within the muscle layers.
The surgeon resumes suturing the serous layer, using the left hand to provide traction and maintain tension on the suture.
The surgeon uses both hands to coordinate and tighten the sutures, demonstrating ambidextrous technique and patience during the suturing process.
The surgeon reaches the opposite end of the suturing line, preparing to terminate the knot. The needle is passed through healthy tissue, and a clot attached to the tail end of the suture is removed.

The surgeon identifies the larger tail end of the suture and prepares to use it as the knotting end. The needle is cut and removed, allowing the needle end to serve as the tail end for easier knot tightening and termination.

The surgeon removes the needle and uses the tail end to create a reverse C knot with two wraps, tightening the first knot securely. A C knot is then formed with two wraps, fed into the Maryland bag due to its small size, and tightened. A final reverse C knot is made with alternating wraps. The suture is cut and removed, followed by the initiation of morselation using the storage morselator.

The surgeon begins the morselation process, consistently pulling the excised tissue to create larger chips. The morselation is a time-consuming procedure, and the process will continue for an extended period before the port is closed.
So thank you very much for watching this video. This was a large intramural myoma removed laparoscopically. Thank you very much. Have a nice day.
